preFix.title: Hand-Lettered Headers
preFix.subtitle: Three treatments: foundational pen lettering, drop-shadow block letter, and watercolour wash with ink outline
preFix.excerpt: Three approaches to heading design for journal pages and paper projects. Foundational pen lettering with a broad nib, drop-shadow constructed block letters in pencil, and a watercolour wash title with pen outline over the top.
preFix.sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on layouts and headings.

content.text: Johnston's foundational approach relies on two things: a steady pen angle and a letter height set in nib widths. Work through the nib-angle exercise in Method 1 before starting any heading. It takes three minutes and prevents the most common mistakes.
Method 1: Foundational pen lettering

The foundational header uses a Mitchell no. 3 nib at a 30-degree pen angle. Fill your nib from the reservoir and make a ladder of horizontal strokes: lay the nib flat and draw short side-by-side strokes. Each one nib-width tall, stacking them vertically. Count five strokes: that stack height is your x-height.
Rule two pencil guide lines: baseline and x-height. Add a cap line 7 nib widths from the baseline for capitals.
Hold the pen so the full width of the nib contacts the paper and the pen angle is constant at 30 to 35 degrees. Do not twist the pen mid-stroke.
Write each heading word slowly in capitals. The broad nib produces its own thick and thin contrast automatically through the pen angle. Do not press harder on downstrokes.
Leave spacing between letters about one stroke width: tight enough to read as a word. Loose enough that letters do not crowd.
Erase pencil guide lines once the ink is fully dry.

attrs.body: If the nib skips or drags, the ink is too thick. Add one drop of water to the ink well and stir. If the nib floods. The reservoir is overfull: touch the underside of the nib to a scrap piece of paper to draw off the excess before returning to the heading.
attrs.heading: Ink flow

content.text: Method 2: Drop-shadow block letter
A
drop-shadow letter
is constructed entirely in pencil first, then outlined in ink. The shadow adds depth without requiring any calligraphy skills.
Rule a baseline and cap line in pencil. The letter height can be whatever suits the page. 8 to 12 mm is typical for a journal header.
Sketch the heading word in plain block capitals between the lines. Keep stroke width consistent: about one-fifth of the cap height.
Decide a shadow direction: 2 mm down and 2 mm to the right is conventional. Lightly mark the offset at three or four anchor points on each letter.
Draw the shadow outline by connecting those offset marks, producing a second letter shape behind each block letter. Only the edges that are behind and below need a line; the front face stays clean.
Fill the shadow shape with hatching or a flat pencil shade. Outline the front block letter in ink using a fountain pen on medium nib.
Erase all pencil construction lines once the ink is dry.
Method 3: Watercolour wash with ink outline
The
watercolour wash title
reverses the usual order: paint first, outline second. The wash must be fully dry before the pen outline is applied.
Sketch the heading word lightly in pencil. Large, open block capitals work best because they give the wash room to show.

Mix a dilute wash in one colour. Load a brush or use a waterbrush pen. Paint within each letter shape, staying about inside the pencil outline. Small bleed outside the lines is fine; the ink outline will correct it.

While the first wash is still damp. Drop a slightly stronger mix of the same colour into the lower half of each letter for a gradient. The wet paper will spread the drop gently. Do not manipulate it further.
Allow to dry completely, at least 15 minutes. The paper may cockle slightly; press flat under a heavy book if needed before proceeding.
Outline each letter shape in ink with a fountain pen on medium nib or a dip pen. Follow the pencil lines closely. The ink outline defines the crisp edge that the wash did not supply.
Erase pencil lines once the ink is dry.
Choosing between the three methods

Foundational lettering is the most demanding technique because the pen angle must be held consistently throughout. It rewards practice and produces headers with authentic calligraphic weight. The drop-shadow method is more forgiving: it is drawn slowly in stages and any error can be corrected with an eraser before the ink stage. The watercolour wash method is the quickest once you are comfortable with a brush and produces the most visually varied results.

items.fix: Flatten the pen so more of the nib edge contacts the paper. Check by drawing a thick horizontal stroke: if the full nib width transfers, the angle is correct.
items.cause: Pen angle is too steep (closer to 90 degrees than 30)
items.symptom: Broad nib produces uniform thin strokes with no contrast

items.fix: Increase the offset to 3 mm. Fill the shadow solidly with hatching in one direction only, darker than the letter face.
items.cause: Shadow offset is too small or the shadow tone matches the letter face
items.symptom: Drop-shadow letters look flat, no depth effect

items.fix: Wait longer before outlining. Test with a dry fingertip on the paper edge: if any coolness is felt, the paper is still holding moisture.
items.cause: Watercolour wash was not fully dry when the ink was applied
items.symptom: Ink outline bleeds into the damp watercolour area
